editorial_angle: The conventional assumption was that AI disruption would work its way up the org chart — bots first, managers later, executives eventually. That model is dead. SaaStr proves the sales floor is already automated at 140% productivity. Adobe proves the CEO chair is already forfeit if you moved too slowly. The squeeze happened simultaneously, not sequentially. There's no tier left that can credibly say 'we'll get to AI later' — the waiting period is over and the market is already scoring the results.
key_message: AI didn't climb the org chart — it hit the sales floor and the corner office at the same time, and the companies still treating it as a roadmap item are already behind.

visual_metaphor_direction.core_tension: A corporate org chart being crushed from both ends simultaneously — AI agents pressing up from the bottom, shareholders and falling stock charts pressing down from the top. The people in the middle are still there, untouched for now, but visibly trapped between two forces that have already decided the outcome.
key_elements: Adobe logo — prominently visible on the corporate building or org chart header
A CEO figure at the top being forced out or ejected upward
Robot / AI agent figures at the base, numerous and organized, doing the work the humans used to do
A descending stock chart or falling share price indicator as the top-down pressure mechanism
The org chart itself as the central structure — a pyramid or hierarchy diagram being crushed in an industrial vise or press
avoid: Humanoid robots that look threatening or sci-fi — keep it corporate and bureaucratic
Layoff imagery (boxes, pink slips) — the story is about replacement structure, not individual job loss
Anything that reads as generic 'AI takes over' — the specificity is the simultaneous top-and-bottom squeeze
Happy or optimistic framing — the mood is reckoning, not celebration
Literal illustration of a sales call or a board meeting — keep it structural and metaphorical

x.hook: AI didn't climb the org chart. It hit both ends at once.
x.angle: SaaStr closed deals with zero humans. Adobe's CEO just got fired for moving too slow. The org chart has no safe tier anymore — that's the story nobody's saying out loud.

linkedin.hook: We've been watching for AI to displace junior roles first. That's not what's happening.
linkedin.angle: SaaStr just posted 140% revenue with 1.25 human salespeople. Same week, Fortune named the Adobe CEO exit the first case of 'CEO AI churn.' If you're a sales leader or a C-suite exec still treating AI as a Q3 roadmap item, this week's numbers are worth a hard look.

bluesky.hook: I keep doing the math on my old company's headcount and I can't make it work anymore.
bluesky.angle: SaaStr posted Q1: 140% of last year's revenue, 1.25 humans in sales, 20+ AI agents. I spent years in FinTech where we ran 30+ people to process $16B annually. I don't know a sales leader who reads this and doesn't immediately recalculate. Then Fortune names Adobe's CEO exit 'CEO AI churn' and it stops being abstract.

do_not_include: Meta 16K layoffs — related theme but makes the post a listicle
YC W26 batch 60% AI companies — too inside-baseball for this angle
Corvera CPG agent revenue — interesting but dilutes the two-story thread
